coredata.dc:title: Synergistic removal of Cr(VI) and dye contaminants by 0D/2D bismuth molybdate homojunction photocatalyst under visible light
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Jingjing
Yue, Junpeng
Niu, Junfeng
Chen, Mindong
coredata.dc:description: Bi2MoO6 quantum dot (QDs-BM)/Bi2MoO6 nanosheet (N-BM) homojunction materials were fabricated by a simple method. Under visible light irradiation, the material exhibits significantly enhanced performance in the synergistic removal of heavy metal Cr(VI) and organic dye (RBY). This can be attributed to the fact that the formation of a homojunction because of energy level difference between QDs-BM and N-BM. It boosts the transferring and separation efficiency of electrons and holes, thereby improving the photocatalytic performance of the material. In addition, compared to the removal of a single heavy metal Cr(VI) contaminant, the mixing of an appropriate amount of organic dye (RBY) greatly increases the reduction efficiency of Cr(VI), and the organic dye is also completely degraded. The results of XPS proved that Cr(VI) is completely reduced to Cr(III). The results also show that the synergistic effect of the oxidation of organic pollutants and the reduction of heavy metals effectively increases the photocatalytic performance of the entire system. This is of great significance for the treatment of industrial wastewater containing heavy metals and organic pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Synergistic effect
Cr(VI)
Organic dye
Bi2MoO6
Homojunction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photocatalytic experiment of single Cr(VI) and Cr(VI)-RBY mixed pollutants

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of the as-prepared (a) and (b) N-BM, (c) QDs-BM, (d) QDs/N-BM materials.
XRD patterns of QDs-BM, N-BM and QDs/N-BM.

(a) Dynamic curve of Cr(VI) reduction under visible light irradiation (λ > 400 nm); (b) Redox dynamic curve of RBY and Cr(VI) mixed pollutants under visible light irradiation (λ > 400 nm); (c) Plots of ln(C0/C) versus time for Cr (VI); (d) Plots of ln(C0/C) versus time for RBY and Cr (VI) mixed pollutants.
(a) Cycling runs for the photodegradation of Cr(VI); (b) Cycling runs for the photodegradation of RBY and Cr (VI) mixed pollutants.
The XRD patterns of 8 wt% QDs/N-BM before and after cyclic test.
(a) Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms; (b) BET specific surface area of different photocatalysts.
(a) The absorption spectra of Cr(VI) versus irradiation time during photocatalysis; (b) The absorption spectra of RBY and Cr(VI) mixed pollutants versus irradiation time during photocatalysis.

(a) Photoluminescence spectra of QDs-BM, N-BM and 8 wt% QDs/N-BM; (b) Transient photocurrent response of QDs-BM, N-BM and QDs/N-BM materials in 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution under the irradiation of visible light (λ > 400 nm).
(a) UV–vis DRS of QDs-BM, N-BM and 8 wt% QDs/N-BM, (b) Plots of (αhν)2 vs hν of QDs-BM and N-BM samples.
(a) VB-XPS spectra of QDs-BM and N-BM samples; (b) Mott–Schottky plots of QDs-BM and N-BM electrodes.
Transfer and separation behaviors of photo-generated charges and photocatalytic reaction mechanism over 8 wt% QDs/N-BM.

head.ce:title: Synergistic removal of Cr(VI) and dye contaminants by 0D/2D bismuth molybdate homojunction photocatalyst under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Atmospheric Environment Monitoring and Pollution Control, School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Collaborative Innovation Center of Atmospheric Environment and Equipment Technology, Jiangsu Engineering Technology Research Center of Environmental Cleaning Materials, Nanjing University of Information Science and Technology, Nanjing, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Atmospheric Environment Monitoring and Pollution Control
School of Environmental Science and Engineering
Collaborative Innovation Center of Atmospheric Environment and Equipment Technology
Jiangsu Engineering Technology Research Center of Environmental Cleaning Materials
Nanjing University of Information Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Eco-Environmental Engineering, Dongguan University of Technology, Dongguan, Guangdong 523808, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bi2MoO6 quantum dot (QDs-BM)/Bi2MoO6 nanosheet (N-BM) homojunction materials were fabricated by a simple method. Under visible light irradiation, the material exhibits significantly enhanced performance in the synergistic removal of heavy metal Cr(VI) and organic dye (RBY). This can be attributed to the fact that the formation of a homojunction because of energy level difference between QDs-BM and N-BM. It boosts the transferring and separation efficiency of electrons and holes, thereby improving the photocatalytic performance of the material. In addition, compared to the removal of a single heavy metal Cr(VI) contaminant, the mixing of an appropriate amount of organic dye (RBY) greatly increases the reduction efficiency of Cr(VI), and the organic dye is also completely degraded. The results of XPS proved that Cr(VI) is completely reduced to Cr(III). The results also show that the synergistic effect of the oxidation of organic pollutants and the reduction of heavy metals effectively increases the photocatalytic performance of the entire system. This is of great significance for the treatment of industrial wastewater containing heavy metals and organic pollutants.

A novel composite photocatalyst with Bi2MoO6 0D quantum dots/2D nanosheets homojunctions was rationally constructed. The obtained sample exhibited significantly enhanced photocatalytic activity for the simultaneous removal of Cr(VI) and dye RBY under visible light irradiation.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal pollution has become a global problem. Among them, the pollution of hexavalent chromium [Cr(VI)] in electroplating, dyestuff and leather manufacturing processes has caused widespread concern [1–3]. Most of the industrial wastewater is discharged directly into the water environment without treatment, causing serious damage to the health of the plants and animals and to the ecosystem. Not only that, Cr(VI) can enter the human body through a variety of ways, causing respiratory lesions, skin allergies, malignancy and so on [4]. Therefore, it is very necessary to deal with Cr(VI) pollution. Photocatalysis is a good treatment method, which can reduce Cr(VI) to less toxic Cr(III) effectively. In addition, different types of contaminants (such as heavy metal and organic pollutants) usually exist simultaneously in industrial wastewater or natureal water body. Therefore, it is imperative to develop an efficient method for the removal of organic contaminants and Cr(VI) simultaneously. There are some works focused on this topic with single component photocatalysts [5–14]. However, the progress is still limited and it is necessary to design novel photocatalytic materials with high performance for the simultaneous removal of organic contaminants and heavy metals.

In recent years, bismuth-based photocatalysts have been extensively studied [15–20]. Among these bismuth-based photocatalysts, Bi2MoO6 is considered as an ideal photocatalyst. Bi2MoO6 has a unique layered structure consisting of oxygen atoms shared [Bi2O2]2+ and perovskite-type layers [21,22]. More importantly, Bi2MoO6 has a suitable valence band and conduction band position [23,24]. Therefore, it is conceivable to achieve photocatalytic oxidation of organic dyes and reduction of Cr(VI) simultaneously [25,26]. However, the high recombination of electrons and holes severely limits the photocatalytic activity of Bi2MoO6 [27–29]. In previous studies, various materials have been used to modify Bi2MoO6 to improve its photocatalytic performance. For example, AgWO4/Ag/Bi2MoO6 [30], Pd/Bi2MoO6 [31], Ag3PO4/Bi2MoO6 [32], Ta3N5/Bi2MoO6 [33], Ag/Bi2MoO6-x [34], Bi2O3/Bi2MoO6 [35], Bi2MoO6/BiOBr [36] with enhanced performance have been prepared. Furthermore, there are few articles prepared Bi2MoO6 homojunction with enhanced performance [26,37]. Bi2MoO6 ultra-thin nanosheets have a unique two-dimensional structure, which can reduce the transmission distance of photo-generated electrons and holes from bulk to surface [38–41]. Therefore, the recombination of photo-generated electrons and holes was reduced and induce to enhanced photocatalytic activity. In addition, it can form a unique zero dimensional (0D)-two dimensional (2D) interface with quantum dots, which further promotes the separation of electrons and holes [42–47]. Furthermore, due to the quantum confinement effect, Bi2MoO6 quantum dots have a larger band gap, which can form a staggered energy level with the Bi2MoO6 nanosheets. Therefore, the Bi2MoO6 nanosheets and quantum dots can form homojunctions, which provide extra separation pathways for photogenerated electron-hole pairs [48–50]. Furthermore, the electrons and holes would transfer to different materials, thus the redox reactions would occur separately. Therefore, it is more suitable for treating mixed pollutants of Cr(VI) and organic pollutants.

In this work, Bi2MoO6 quantum dots (QDs-BM) and Bi2MoO6 nanosheets (N-BM) have been combined to form 0D/2D homojunction composite photocatalysts. Careful characterization of the morphology, phase structure, electronic and optical properties were carried out. The photocatalytic activity of the homojunction material was evaluated by the simultaneous reduction of Cr(VI) and degradation of Reactive Brilliant Yellow under visible light irradiation.

Synthesis method of QDs-BM. Firstly, 1.6 mmol of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O is dispersed in 80 mL sodium oleate (8.8 mmol) aqueous solution to form solution A. Meanwhile, 1.6 mmol Na2MoO4·2H2O was dispersed in 80 mL of deionized water to form solution B. Afterwards, solution B was slowly added to solution A to form solution C, which was stirred for 2 h. Finally, solution C was added into a 200 mL Teflon autoclave and then treated at 180 °C for 18 h. After naturally cooling, the product was collected, centrifuged and washed several times with alcohol and n-hexane. Finally, the product was dried at 60 °C for 24 h.

For the synthesis of N-BM, 4 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and 2 mmol Na2MoO4·2H2O were added to 160 mL deionized water with vigorous stirring, and then 0.2 mmol of KBr was added to the above solution and stirred for 30 min. The mixed solution was poured into a 200 mL Teflon autoclave and incubated at 150 °C for 24 h. After naturally cooling, the product was collected, centrifuged and washed several times with deionized water. Finally, the product was dried at 60 °C for 24 h.

The QDs/N composite was synthesized by ultrasonic dispersion and mechanical agitation. In the synthesis of the materials, a certain amount of QDs-BM and 0.2 g N-BM were respectively dispersed in 30 mL of ethanol (QDs-BM was added in an amount of 2 wt%, 5 wt%, 8 wt%, 10 wt%), and they were uniformly dispersed by ultrasonication for 30 min. Then, the alcohol solution containing QDs-BM was slowly poured into the N-BM solution under vigorous stirring, and after stirring for 1 h, it was centrifuged and dried in air at 60 °C. The product obtained was defined as 2 wt% QDs/N, 5 wt% QDs/N, 8 wt% QDs/N, 10 wt% QDs/N.

The phase structure of the prepared sample was characterized by X-ray diffractometer (XRD-6100). The UV–vis absorption spectrum was tested by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU UV-2450). Photoluminescence spectra were detected by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent USA). The morphologies were observed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-200cx). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Electron, ESCALAB 250 Xi., USA).

Photocurrent testing was performed using an electrochemical workstation (CHI 660D Chenhua Instrument Company) under visible light illumination. During the photocurrent measurement, the Pt wire was used as the counter electrode and the Ag/AgCl was used as the reference electrode. FTO conductive glasses deposited with different samples were served as the working electrode. The electrolyte was a 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution. In addition, a Mott-Schottky curve was measured with a frequency of 1 kHz.

In this experiment, 50 mL of a mixed solution containing potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7, 10 ppm) and reactive brilliant yellow (BRY, 0.3 ppm, 1 ppm, 2 ppm, 5 ppm, 10 ppm) was poured into a 100 mL beaker and then 30 mg sample was added. In order to achieve an adsorption-desorption balance between the contaminant and the photocatalyst, the system was stirred in the dark for 30 min before irradiation. During visible light irradiation (CEL-HXF300, Beijing AuLight), 3 mL of the mixture was sampled every 20 min and the Cr(VI) concentration was measured with an ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-6100).

The morphology and structure of the N-BM, QDs-BM and QDs/N-BM materials were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. 1a–c). Fig. 1a shows the TEM images of N-BM. It can be clearly seen that Bi2MoO6 is in a sheet-like structure with uniform dispersion and smooth surface area (the inset showed the size distribution of N-BM). Fig. 1b shows a TEM image of N-BM with higher resolution. It can be seen that there is no any small particles on the smooth surface. Fig. 1c shows a typical TEM image of QDs-BM having a particle size of about 5 nm, and an electron diffraction pattern (SAED) image in the upper right corner of Fig. 1b shows that QDs-BM has polycrystalline properties. As can be seen from Fig. 1d, a large number of QDs-BM are uniformly attached to the surface of the N-BM. The results indicate the successful formation of the 0D/2D interface between QDs-BM and N-BM.

The crystal phase of the samples was investigated by XRD. In Fig. 2 , the diffraction peaks of N-BM appear at 10.9°, 28.2°, 32.6°, 33.1°, 36.0°, 46.7°, 47.1°, 55.5°, 56.2° and 58.4° corresponding to (020), (131), (002), (060), (151), (202), (062)/(260), (331)/(133), (191) and (262) planes, which are attributed to the orthorhombic Phase Bi2MoO6 (JCPDS No. 76-2388) [51]. No other miscellaneous peaks were detected, indicating the successful synthesis of BM series materials. Further, at the 28.2° diffraction peak, the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of QDs-BM and N-BM was 1.43 and 0.32. According to the Scherrer formula (d = 0.9λ/(βcosθ)) the crystallite sizes of QDs-BM and N-BM are calculated to be approximately 5.7 nm and 25.7 nm. This difference in crystallite size results in all diffraction peaks of QDs-BM being broad and short [52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic experiment of single Cr(VI) and Cr(VI)-RBY mixed pollutants
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the photocatalytic performance of different photocatalysts was evaluated by Cr(VI) reduction under the irradiation of visible light. Before the photocatalytic degradation reaction, the photocatalysts and pollutant solution were agitated in the dark for 30 min to obtain an absorption-desorption equilibrium. From the dynamic curve of Cr(VI) reduction in Fig. 3a, QDs-BM exhibited poorer adsorption capacity than pure N-BM due to the agglomeration effect of quantum dots. After the deposition of quantum dots, the adsorption capacity of composite samples was also deteriorated, which may be due to the fact that quantum dots occupying the adsorption sites on the surface of N-BM. However, all composite samples exhibited higher photocatalytic performance than pure QDs-BM and N-BM. It is proved that the introduction of quantum dots greatly improves the photocatalytic performance of N-BM. In particular, sample modified with 8 wt% quantum dots exhibited the highest photocatalytic activity. It can be ascribed to the following factor. The active sites would increase with the increasing of the deposited QDs in a certain region. And 8 wt% is the most suitable value. Exceeding this value causes the aggregation of QDs. Therefore, the QDs cannot form junctions with nanosheets effectively and induce to the deterioration of photocatalytic activity. Fig. 3c shows the relationship between ln(C0/C) and the irradiation time. It can be seen from the figure that the introduction of quantum dots increases the s. It can be seen that 8 wt% QDs/N-BM exhibited the highest apparent rate constant (0.024 min−1), which is about 4 times as that of N-BM (0.006 min−1).

In order to better simulate industrial wastewater, we add a certain concentration of organic pollutants - RBY, while maintaining a constant Cr(VI) concentration. We suspect that organic pollutants can fully utilize the h+ generated in the photocatalytic process, inhibit the recombination of electrons and holes, and promote the reduction of Cr(VI). We use a 8 wt% QDs/N-BM sample for photocatalytic testing of the mixed solution. Excitingly, the introduction of RBY does increase the reduction rate of Cr(VI). To further explore the effect of RBY concentration on the reduction rate of Cr (VI), we changed the concentration of RBY in the system to be 0.3 ppm, 1 ppm, 2 ppm, 5 ppm, 10 ppm. The kinetic curve of Cr(VI) reduction is shown in Fig. 3b. Regardless of the concentration of RBY, the reduction rate of Cr(VI) has a certain increase. Fig. 3d shows the relationship between ln(C0/C) and the irradiation time. As can be seen from the figure, the introduction of RBY accelerates the reduction rate of Cr(VI). Cr(VI) has the fastest reduction rate (0.05 min−1) when a RBY solution with a concentration of 1 ppm was added, which is approximately 2.5 times that of Cr (VI) (0.02 min−1). Considering that Kr2Cr2O7 has strong oxidizing properties, we conducted corresponding control experiments and found that Cr(VI) did not directly react with BRY.

Fig. 6a shows the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm curves for QDs-BM, N-BM and 8 wt% QDs/N. Their SBET is shown in Fig. 6b. The SBET values are 4.8141, 15.1588 and 13.4021 m2/g for QDs, N-BM, and 8 wt% QDs/N, respectively. This confirms that the quantum dots occupy some adsorption site on the surface of the N-BM, and the adsorption capacity of the composite sample also deteriorates.

Fig. 7 shows the variation of UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) along with the irradiation time in two different systems. Fig. 7a shows the variation of absorption spectrum of pure Cr(VI) solution. It can be seen that the intensity of the Cr(VI) absorption peak decreases gradually with the increase of the irradiation time. After 2 h of irradiation, the intensity of the Cr(VI) absorption peak dropped to zero, indicating that Cr(VI) has been reduced to Cr(III). Fig. 7b shows the absorption spectra of mixed solutions of Cr(VI) and RBY (1 ppm). Before the beginning of the light irradiation, the characteristic absorption peak of Cr(VI) at 350 nm hardly changed, while the absorption peak near 425 nm slightly increased, which is due to RBY. As the irradiation time increased, we found that Cr(VI) was largely reduced in the first 20 min of illumination, and Cr(VI) was completely reduced to Cr(III) in 80 min. At the same time, the absorption peak of RBY at 425 nm also dropped to zero, indicating that RBY has also been fully degraded.

In order to better analyze the photocatalytic products of single Cr(VI) pollutants and Cr(VI)-RBY mixed pollutants. The 8 wt% of QDs/N-BM materials were collected after the reaction, and the Cr attached to its surface was analyzed by XPS. Obviously, there are two peaks of Cr 2p3/2 and Cr 2p1/2 peaks of Cr(III) at 576.9 eV and 586.5 eV (Fig. 8 ) [14,53,54], similar to the binding energy of Cr(III) in Cr(III)xOy and Cr(OH)3. In addition, we did not observe any peak of Cr(VI) [55,56], which means that Cr(VI) in solution has been completely reduced to Cr(III).

The photoluminescence (PL) measurements and electrochemical experiments are commonly used to explore the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Fig. 9a shows PL spectra of QDs-BM, N-BM and 8 wt% QD/N-BM materials, and Fig. 9b shows their photocurrent-time (I-t) curves as electrodes. It can be inferred from the two figures that the electron-hole separation efficiency of the QDs/N-BM material is higher than that of the corresponding single QDs-BM or N-BM material. This indicates that the existing 0D/2D homojunction structures formed by QDs-BM and N-BM promoted electron-hole pair transfer and separation [59].

Fig. 10 (a) shows the DRS for QDs-BM, N-BM and 8 wt% QDs/N-BM. As shown, all samples have a certain absorption in the visible range, and the absorption edges of QDs-BM and N-BM are located near 470 nm and 490 nm, respectively. Enhanced absorbance was observed in the visible region for the 8 wt% QDs/N-BM materials compared to N-BM. This increase in absorptive capacity indicates that more electron-hole pairs would be generated. To further explore the structure, the relative conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) positions of the QDs-BM and N-BM samples were measured and calculated. Fig. 10b shows the (αhν)2 and hv curves obtained according to the Kubelka-Munk method and the Mott-Schottky curves obtained by using QDs-BM and N-BM samples as electrodes [57]. The band gap of QDs-BM (2.91 eV) increases compared to N-BM (2.75 eV), which is caused by the quantum confinement effect [58]. Fig. 11a shows the XPS valence band spectra of QDs-BM and N-BM. It can be seen that the values are 2.34 and 2.27 V for N-BM and QDs-BM, respectively. It means that the energy gap between VB positions and flat band potentials are 2.34 and 2.27 V for N-BM and QDs-BM, respectively [59]. Fig. 11b shows the Mott-Schottky plots of N-BM and QDs-BM. The flat band potentials (Efb) of QDs-BM and N-BM are estimated to be −0.75 and −0.55 V versus Ag/AgCl electrode, which correspond to −0.55 and −0.35 V with respect to the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE), respectively. Therefore, the VB positions can be calculated to be 1.99 and 1.72 eV for N-BM and QDs-BM, respectively [60]. The CB positions can also be calculated to be −0.76 and −1.19 for N-BM and QDs-BM, respectively.

According to the above measurement and calculation results, it can be seen that there is a significant difference in energy levels between QDs-BM and N-BM. The formation of such staggered levels facilitates the transfer and separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, thereby increasing photocatalytic activity. Photoluminescence measurements and electrochemical experiments have also demonstrated this (Fig. 12 ). In addition, the required potential of Cr(VI)/Cr(III) is 1.33 eV (vs NHE) [53,61], and the Bi2MoO6 series of materials we prepared have a more negative potential, indicating that our materials have the ability to reduce Cr(VI) to Cr (III). The photocatalytic mechanism is shown in Fig. 11 and the photocatalytic processes are:
(1)
QDs / N − BM + hv →
h +
VB
+
e −
CB
(2)
Cr 2
O 7
2 −
+ 14
H +
+
6e −
→
2Cr
3 +
+
7H 2
O
(3)
O 2
+
e −
→ ˙
O 2
−
(4)
˙
O 2
−
/
h +
+ RBY → degraded products

It can be seen that a single Cr(VI) pollutant can only use the electrons generated by the conduction band, and the holes generated by the valence band not only have no use value, but even inhibit the reaction between electrons and Cr(VI), resulting in the rate of chromium reduction to be very slow. Therefore, the addition of organic pollutants is very necessary. The addition of RBY makes full use of holes, which promotes the separation of electron-hole pairs, and the reduction rate of Cr(VI) is greatly improved. In addition, QDs-BM and N-BM form a homojunction, which realizes the spatial separation of electron-hole pairs, and the redox reaction occurs at different positions. The uniformly distributed QD provides a large number of photocatalytic reaction sites, which further increases the Cr(VI) reduction rate.

Firstly, the QDs/N-BM homojunction composites are successfully prepared. The reduction experiments of Cr(VI) under visible light irradiation proved that the self-modification by quantum dots can promote the transfer and separation efficiency of electrons and holes, which enhances photocatalytic activity. More importantly, we used the RBY-Cr (VI) mixed solution to simulate actual industrial wastewater, and explored the effect of different concentrations of RBY on the reduction rate of Cr(VI). The relatively low concentration of RBY has a significant effect on the reduction of Cr(VI). When the RBY concentration is 1 ppm, the promotion effect is the best and the RBY is also sufficiently degraded. This is of great significance for the application of photocatalytic technology in the treatment of industrial wastewater containing Cr(VI) and organic dyes.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for grants from the Guangdong Innovation Team Project for Colleges and Universities (No. 2016KCXTD023), and Guangdong Province Universities and Colleges Pearl River Scholar Funded Scheme (2017), the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).
